name: Aztlan
description: Aztlan is the ancestral homeland of the Aztec peoples, including the Mexica. It is often described as an island or coastal region located to the northwest of the Valley of Mexico. According to Aztec mythology, the Mexica migrated from Aztlan, guided by their patron deity Huitzilopochtli, after being instructed to seek a new home where they would find an eagle perched on a cactus devouring a serpent. The exact location of Aztlan remains a mystery, with some scholars suggesting it was a real place while others view it as a purely mythical origin point. Its significance lies in its role as the symbolic source of Aztec identity, legitimacy, and cultural heritage. The journey from Aztlan represents a transformative passage, shaping the Mexica into the powerful empire they would become. The legends surrounding Aztlan highlight themes of migration, divine guidance, and the establishment of a new order.
subtitle: The mythical homeland of the Aztecs.

keyMyths.title: The Migration from Aztlan
keyMyths.description: The Mexica, guided by Huitzilopochtli, left Aztlan to find a new home. They were instructed to look for an eagle devouring a serpent on a cactus, which would mark the location of their future capital, Tenochtitlan.

keyMyths.title: Aztlan's Paradise
keyMyths.description: Some accounts describe Aztlan as a paradise-like place of abundance. The reasons for the Mexica's departure from this idyllic homeland are varied and often tied to divine will or internal conflicts.

sources.author: Miguel León-Portilla
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Aztec philosophy, religion, and history.
sources.text: A comprehensive overview of Aztec philosophy, religion, and history.

sources.author: David Carrasco
sources.description: Explores the religious beliefs and practices of various Mesoamerican cultures, including the Aztecs.
